dialogue.utterance: Rich EMS, Unit 3 dispatched for a 45-year-old male priority 1 for breathing problems at Rite Aid parking lot.
Hi, my name is Jordan, an EMT with [STATE] Rescue. What made you call 911 today?
Ah, I've been having trouble breathing all day with cough and chest congestion. It wasn't getting any better.
Can you tell me your name and where we are right now?
My name is Matthew, and we're in the ambulance.
What day is today and what do you think the time is?
It must be Friday, maybe 7 o'clock in the evening.
And can you rephrase why you called 911?
I had chest congestion and breathing problems all day with no relief.
Eyes open and no visible trauma.
Mr. Matthew, can you describe how you are feeling?
I feel tightness in the upper chest and trouble catching my breath.
Can you push on our hands with both hands?
<pushes>
Airway is patent and well-maintained with no obstruction or signs of airway compromise.
Permission to listen to your lungs for a moment?
Sure, go ahead.
I hear bilateral wheezing in upper and lower lobes. Breathing at normal rate and depth.
We'll check your pulse now. Is that okay with you?
Yes, proceed.
Strong radial pulse at 108 beats per minute. No irregularities detected.
Respirations at 14 per minute with normal depth and no signs of increased work of breathing noted on initial assessment.
Blood pressure is 117 over 79 with regular rhythm and no peripheral edema observed.
Blood glucose is 118-within normal limits initially. No hyperglycemia reported by the patient or observed symptoms to suggest it.
SpO2 is at 93%. Still well oxygenated but low enough to consider supplemental oxygen as needed or advised by protocols.
You mentioned breathing problems, anything else you're experiencing or changes in the last 24 hours?
Just some chest congestion, no pain, nausea, or vomiting reported. No active bleeding or trauma to the chest.
May we ask about your medical history for record-keeping purposes?
I have asthma, hypertension, and a diagnosis of schizoaffective disorder recorded in my chart. All under stable management prior to today's episode.
Any medications you're currently on at home or specifically using today to manage symptoms like the ones we just discussed?
I use albuterol and take Norvasc. I had not used the albuterol today.
Any medication allergies we should be aware of to avoid cross-reaction with any potential rescue treatment?
I react to ziprasidone, haloperidol, and valproate, as listed in my affirmed allergy history. Nobody gave those meds today.
What was the last you had to eat or drink, and do you use any substances that affect breathing or oxygen exchange today?
Had some alcohol about 25 minutes prior to us arriving on scene. No cigarette or food in the last hour.
When did the breathing issues start today, and was any activity associated with it when it first appeared?
I've had them since around 10 a.m. He stayed standing when we found him, no fall or injury report.
On a scale of 0 to 10, how much discomfort do you have, and, just to clarify, is that chest tightness or pain?
0, there's no pain, just breathing difficulties. Maybe in the tightness it feels like pressure in the upper and mid-chest.
Anything that makes it better or worse?
Chest feels tight when I inhale. Deep breaths don't help. Maybe sitting up helps a little.
Skin is warm and dry with normal color, no desaturation under skin, no burns or rashes found.
May we check your throat or ears for obstructions or drainage?
No problem.
No obstruction or drainage observed.
Can we test your neck for stiffness or evidence of trauma in the spino-cervical region on our initial evaluation?
Sure.
No neck stiffness, swollen glands, or head trauma observed.
Might we listen again to your lungs for reproducibility and guidance in next steps?
Certainly.
Still wheezing bilaterally in both upper and lower lung fields on re-auscultation.
May we do a quick belly check to assess for abdominal causes contributing to diaphragm dysfunction?
Go ahead.
Abdomen is soft, no tenderness or masses detected.
We need to check your legs and arms-comfortable with that?
Yes.
No deformities or wounds found. Peripheral pulses present at distal and proximal, warm and well-perfused extremities.
May we place leads for your heartbeat for monitoring under the acute episode?
EKG shows a sinus tachycardia at 108 initially, matching your heart rate with the machine.
Let me recheck the blood glucose. The first time was okay-118 with normal reading and now, maybe the machine may be off.
Field impression is asthma exacerbation due to active wheezing, reactive airway disease likely causing respiratory distress, all per the field medical protocol.
We're giving you a DuoNeb with 5 mg albuterol and 0.5 mg Atrovent, both per your medical status and known protocol.
<administers nebulizer>
It feels better, no strange reaction at all recorded as of now.
Blood pressure now is 126 over 90.
Breathing rate back to 11 with a comfortable pattern, less work of breathing noted on repeat assessment.
Heart rate decreased to 104, stable and not in acute distress.
Oxygen saturation improved to 98%-very good progress and per hospital guidelines.
Rechecked blood glucose, it still shows 0-likely a monitor malfunction as already reviewed.
We continue under asthma protocol due to improved vitals and respiratory status indicators on the monitor.
How is your breathing feeling after this initial medication, Mr. Matthew?
Still a little wheeze when I inhale, but definitely better now.
We're going to give another albuterol puff and dexamethasone IV to address inflammation and see further improvement.
<administers second nebulizer and dexamethasone>
You're breathing better now and staying stable overall according to the ECG.
We recommend transporting you to [COMPANY_NAME]-would that work for your main hospital?
[COMPANY_NAME] is fine.
Transport is non-emergency to [COMPANY_NAME]-approximately ten minutes arrival with minimal traffic.
You are secured with a five-point harness. Discomfort noted?
Yep, all set and well.
ETCO2 is 47, waveform looks square-good sign in non-critical tachycardia with CO2 in normal range.
GCS of 15-fully alert and oriented to person, time, place, and event with no short-term confusion.
4-lead ECG is placed and stable on the monitor, per transfer protocol.
Keep ECG on board and heart rate stable for entire transport as per standard of care.
Cleaned IV line with a 10 mL flush after dexamethasone.
Recheck blood glucose shows 0 again-monitor likely not functioning properly but not impacting clinical care.
Found you in parking lot of Rite Aid-no reported falls or injuries in incident.
No other symptoms or examinations changed en route consistent with baseline and no trauma at initial interaction on arrival.
Transport is proceeding smoothly to [COMPANY_NAME] as planned with constant monitoring for any changes.
We will protect your dignity and safety the entire way in the process of transport.
Let us know if you need anything else in the four seats we've sanitized and ready to go.
Nope, I think that is all-thank you for checking in.
No changes; pulse remains 104 and strong at the exit.
No issue on respirations, smooth at 11 per minute of average breath volume.
BP is 126 over 90-still stable and within normal range.
SpO2 at 98%-very good signal and waveform quality for those watching screens back at base.
Glucose still 0-monitor likely not accurate but not in active partition of patient care at this moment.
Patient in good stable condition en route and ready for delivery at intake.
Let's go through the hospital receiving line now-[COMPANY_NAME] is alerted by RMS with all patient info to assess.
All protocol is in place and no concerns for transport safety under plan of management.
